title: God's Gift of Salvation
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 12-22-24 -AM- God's Gift of Salvation - John 3_9-21 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Gift That Started Christmas
subpoints: Gift-giving traditions at Christmas trace to: the Magi bringing gifts to Jesus; legends of Nicholas of Myra; the Roman Saturnalia and Norse Yule; but most directly in modern Western practice to Queen Victoria and Prince Albert
Yet God is the one who started the tradition of giving that Christmas celebrates — he gave the gift that started Christmas altogether
John 3 is not a historical account of the birth but a theological explanation for the need of the Incarnation — why it was necessary for God the Son to take flesh
The title "God's Gift of Salvation" is purposely ambiguous: both the gift that is Christ (gift = Christ) and the gift Christ gives (Christ as giver)

outline.title: 1. It Starts with God (John 3:16)
subpoints: The first word in the original Greek of verse 16 is not "for" but "God" — God stands at the beginning, not us; Christmas starts with God because the gospel starts with God

Even in the midst of the curse in Genesis 3:15, God says "I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your seed and her Seed; he shall bruise your head" — from the very beginning God says I will save you
Left to ourselves we would never come to God; Romans 5:8-10 — God demonstrates his love toward us in that while we were still sinners Christ died for us; while we were enemies we were reconciled
Ephesians 2:4-5 — God who is rich in mercy, even when we were dead in trespasses, made us alive together with Christ
Sinners, enemies, dead — God is the one orchestrating everything; if the gospel starts with us, we have no hope

outline.title: 2. God Loved in This Manner (John 3:16a)
subpoints: The word "so" in "God so loved" does not indicate the magnitude of love but the manner of love — not how much but what kind
God's love is a giving love — defined and demonstrated by giving what is most precious to him: his Son; "so" = "in this manner" or "in this way"
For that moment on the cross God separated himself from his Son — something that had never happened before; the entire intensity of hell was felt by Christ on the cross
Romans 8:32 — he who did not spare his own Son but delivered him up for us all, how shall he not with him also freely give us all things?
The love of Christ for his church is also defined by giving himself for her (Ephesians 5:25)

God loved the world — not one kind of people over another; racism is an egregious sin because it is contrary to who God is; Jesus came not only for Jews but for all nations, all ethnicities; heaven in Revelation shows a gathering of all kinds
"World" also means Christ is the only Savior — not one option among many; the gospel is the unbabeling of Babel

outline.title: 3. The Gift Itself: God's Only Begotten Son
subpoints: "Only begotten Son" — Jesus is unlike any other being in the universe; not the brother of Satan (Mormonism) or the angel Gabriel (Jehovah's Witnesses)
We are adopted sons and daughters; Jesus is the eternal Son — the only one who is both man and God in one person in this unique way
Psalm 84:11 — the Lord God is a sun and shield; no good thing will he withhold from those who walk uprightly; God only gives us the best; Christ is the best Savior
The result of God's giving love: rescue from destruction (the word "perish" = destruction) and everlasting life

The world is already condemned in verse 18 — sin has separated humanity from God and apart from Christ they are under judgment (John 3:36: the wrath of God abides on the one who does not believe — present tense)

The attractiveness of eternal life is not its length (all moral beings live forever) but its quality — full life in the loving presence of God (Psalm 16); fellowship with the people of God; beholding Jesus as he is (1 John 3:1-2)

outline.title: 4. The Difference: Faith (John 3:16b)
subpoints: The only difference between those who perish and those who have eternal life is faith — not who they are, not their accomplishments

Faith has three parts: (1) knowledge — Romans 10: faith comes by hearing the word of God; (2) assent — intellectual conviction that the knowledge is true and corresponds to one's need; (3) trust — conviction passing into confidence, receiving Christ's work from his hands
2 Timothy 1:12 — I know whom I have believed and am persuaded he is able to keep what I have committed to him
We need to believe two things: who Christ is (God and man) and what Christ did (obeyed the law on our behalf, suffered the punishment of sin, rose as the Father's joyful acceptance of his sacrifice)

God sees believers as he sees Christ; not our efforts, not our obedience, not our faithfulness — Jesus the perfect Son; so his love for us never changes; on your worst day God loves you the same as on your best day
The worst sin is unbelief — verse 18: condemnation is for the one who has not believed in the name of the only begotten Son; the only sin that seals one's destiny in hell

summary: A Christmas Sunday morning sermon on John 3:9-21, built around the single most famous verse in the Bible. Pastor Lyro situates the passage in its context: Nicodemus, the premier Jewish teacher, comes to Jesus by night like someone creeping along the wallpaper, unwilling to be seen associating with this preacher from Galilee. Jesus uses mild sarcasm — "you are the teacher of Israel and do not know these things?" — to prick his heart, then explains who he is and why he came. The sermon walks through John 3:16 word by word in the original Greek: God stands first (Christmas starts with God, not us); "so" indicates the manner of love not its extent (a giving love, not just a large love); "the world" means all nations and all ethnicities without distinction (making racism contrary to who God is) and means Christ is the only Savior; "only begotten Son" identifies Jesus as unlike any being — God and man in one person; the result is rescue from destruction and everlasting life (whose attractiveness lies not in length but in quality — full life with God and with Christ). The sermon closes with the three elements of saving faith — knowledge, assent, and trust — illustrated from 2 Timothy 1:12, and with the warning that the worst sin is unbelief, the one sin that seals one's eternal destiny.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Gift That Started Christmas

I never met somebody who is anti-Christmas who does not like getting gifts. Whatever they think about Christmas, a gift — okay, thank you.

The tradition of gift-giving at Christmas has several strands. Some of it comes from the Magi bringing gifts to Jesus (they were still coming months after his birth — if you have a manger scene, put the Magi a little distance away). Some comes from Nicholas of Myra, the fourth-century bishop who secretly gave gifts to the poor by breaking into houses at night and putting gifts in the socks hanging to dry. That sounds familiar. Another strand comes from the Roman Saturnalia and the Norse Yule celebrating the winter solstice. And the biggest modern influence is Queen Victoria and Prince Albert, who revived Christmas observance in England and especially emphasized giving gifts to children.

But it is also appropriate to say that God is the one who started the tradition of giving that Christmas. He gave the gift that started Christmas — the Lord Jesus Christ. And that is what our passage tells us today.

John 3 is not a historical account of the birth of Christ — no shepherds, no angels, no Magi, no annunciation, no Magnificat. It is a theological explanation for the need of the Incarnation — why it was necessary for God the Son to become a son of men.

The title "God's Gift of Salvation" is purposely ambiguous. It can mean the gift that is Christ (gift = Christ). Or it can mean that Christ is the one giving the gift. The Bible in this chapter teaches both truths.

## Setting: Nicodemus Comes by Night

Why is Jesus saying what he is saying here in John 3? Nicodemus, a leader of the Jews — a member of the Sanhedrin — came to visit Jesus by night. John uses a construction in the original language that tells us Nicodemus was doing the kind of action that is done only by night. Like creeping along the wallpaper trying not to be seen — he did not want anybody to know.

He had seen and heard enough about Jesus to make him wonder if something special was happening. But he was not yet ready to commit. And he came quietly because a connection with this preacher from Galilee would be a black eye on his reputation.

In verse 2 he says: "Rabbi, we know that you are a teacher come from God, for no one can do these signs that you do unless God is with him." He had some knowledge of Jesus, but it was nowhere near enough. He did not have a clue about who Jesus really was or what his mission was.

So in verse 10, Jesus asks with gentle sarcasm: "Are you the teacher of Israel and do not know these things?" Nicodemus at the time was the premier Jewish teacher — everyone in Palestine knew about him. And Jesus says: you are the teacher, and you do not know these things? Jesus then explains who he is and what he was sent to do, in verses 14-17: as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up; God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son.

## 1. It Starts with God

In the original Greek, the first word of verse 16 is God. In Greek the word "for" is never the first word of a sentence — it is what is called postpositive. The first word is God. And that makes sense, because Christmas starts with God. The gospel itself starts with God — not with us, not with you and me, but with God himself.

Even in the midst of cursing the serpent in Genesis 3:15 — in the very moment of judging sin — God says: *I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your seed and her Seed; he shall bruise your head.* Right there at the beginning of time, God says: I will save you. I will do this. It is my Son through the woman who is going to crush the serpent's head.

Left to ourselves, we would never move toward God. Romans 5:8-10: *God demonstrates his own love toward us in that while we were still sinners Christ died for us.* Not while we were good little people. While we were still sinners. While we were enemies, we were reconciled to God through the death of his Son.

Ephesians 2:4-5: *But God, who is rich in mercy, because of his great love with which he loved us, even when we were dead in trespasses, made us alive together with Christ.* Sinners. Enemies. Dead. God is the one orchestrating everything. If the gospel starts with us, we have no hope.

## 2. God Loved in This Manner

God so loved the world. The word "so" here does not indicate how much God loves us. It indicates in what manner God loves us. You could replace it: God in this manner loved the world. God loved the world — in what way? In the way of giving his Son.

God's love is a giving type of love. Defined and demonstrated by giving, by sending his Son. God's love is defined by giving us what is most precious to him. For that moment on the cross, God separated himself from his Son — something that had never happened before. The entire intensity of hell was felt by the Lord Jesus Christ as he hung on the cross. That is the kind of love we see here. A love that gives what is most precious. A love that saves.

Romans 8:32 — *he who did not spare his own Son but delivered him up for us all — how shall he not with him also freely give us all things?* That is the kind of God you have. A God who does not hold anything back from you.

Not only is the love of God the Father defined by his giving; the love of Jesus for us is also defined by giving himself for us. Ephesians 5:25 — husbands, love your wives, just as Christ loved the church and gave himself for her.

God loved the world. The love of God is not pre-eminent toward one kind of people over another. He does not love the Americans because he preferred Americans, or the Jews because he preferred Jews. His love transcends ethnicity. And that is why when we see heaven in Revelation, we see a gathering made of all kinds of ethnicities. The gospel is the un-babeling of Babel — where men tried to reach God by their own efforts and God scattered them; the gospel brings all nations back together.

Jesus did not come for all peoples without exception — otherwise everyone would be saved. But he came for all peoples without distinction. And another reason for the word "world" is to make clear that Christ is the only Savior — not one option among many.

## 3. God's Only Begotten Son

God gave his only begotten Son. Jesus is like no other being in the universe. Not the brother of Satan, not the angel Gabriel. We are adopted sons and daughters of God. Jesus is the eternal Son — the only one who is both man and God in one person in that unique way.

God only gives us the best. Psalm 84:11 — *the Lord God is a sun and a shield; the Lord will give grace and glory; no good thing will he withhold from those who walk uprightly.* God only gives us the best. Therefore Christ is the best Savior.

The result of this giving love: rescue from destruction and everlasting life. The word "perish" is the word for destruction. And Jesus tells us in verse 18 that the world is already condemned because of sin — it is only a matter of time before the full force of God's judgment is manifested. John 3:36: *the wrath of God abides on the one who does not believe in the Son.* Not will abide — abides. Present tense.

But rescue from destruction and everlasting life. The attractiveness of eternal life is not its length — all moral beings, all angels and all humans, will live forever in some form. Every soul has a beginning but no soul has an end. The attractiveness of eternal life is the kind of life it is: life in the loving presence of God, full life (Psalm 16 — *in your presence is fullness of joy*), fellowship with the people of God, life beholding Jesus as he is (1 John 3:1-2 — *we shall be like him, for we shall see him as he is*).

## 4. The Difference: Faith

What is the difference between those who perish and those who have everlasting life? One thing: *whoever believes in him*. Not who they are, not their accomplishments. Just faith.

Faith has three parts. First: knowledge. Romans 10 — faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the word of God. John 20 — John wrote his Gospel so that with these facts you may believe that Jesus is the Son of God. So the first part of believing is knowing.

Second: assent — the intellectual conviction that the knowledge you have acquired about Christ is indeed factually true, and that the provisions of the gospel correspond exactly to your need. You can know the gospel but not believe it is true. That is not saving faith.

Third: trust — assent passing from conviction into confidence. Christ did all these things for me, and I receive it from his hands. You know it, you believe it is true, and you trust in it. 2 Timothy 1:12: *I know whom I have believed and am persuaded that he is able to keep what I have committed to him until that day.*

We need to believe two things. First, who Christ is: both man and God in one person — Son of God and Son of Man. Second, what Christ did: in his life he obeyed the requirements of the law of God on behalf of those who could not obey; in his death he suffered the punishment for the sins of his people, separated from his Father; in his resurrection the Father joyfully accepted the sacrifice his Son offered on behalf of all whom the Son represents.

God sees you as he sees Christ — not your efforts, not your obedience, not how faithfully you followed him, not how little you sinned — but Jesus the perfect Son. Therefore God's love for you never changes. At your worst day, God loves you the same as at your best day.

Stop looking within yourself for a reason for the love of God. Look outside yourself to Christ — that is where the face of the love of God is seen.

And friends who do not believe: the worst sin you can commit is unbelief. Verse 18: the one who does not believe is condemned already because he has not believed in the name of the only begotten Son of God. That is the one sin that seals one's eternal destiny.

God in his gracious disposition toward you brought you here today to hear the truth of salvation in Jesus Christ. Do not discard that. Today is the day of salvation. Receive it, assent to it, trust in the Lord Jesus Christ.
